title: <div>  2.  Change the setting – Place it in a wildly different environment.</div
content:
2. Change the setting – Place it in a wildly different environment.
3. Change the protagonist – What if they were younger, older, from a different background?
4. Reverse the genre – Turn a drama into a thriller, a tragedy into a comedy.
5. Flip the conflict – What if the antagonist was the protagonist?

SECTION 3: STRUCTURING YOUR STORY (Multiple Approaches)

Once you have your idea, it’s time to choose a structure that best fits your story.

Option 1: Three-Act Structure
• Act 1: Introduce protagonist, world, and inciting incident.
• Act 2: Conflict escalates, protagonist struggles, major turning point.
• Act 3: Climax and resolution.

Option 2: The Hero’s Journey
• Ordinary World → Call to Adventure → Refusal → Mentor Appears → Crossing the Threshold → Challenges and Growth → Ordeal → Transformation → Return with Knowledge.

Option 3: Kishōtenketsu (Japanese Structure)
• Introduction (Ki): Establish world and characters.
• Development (Shō): Deepen understanding of the situation.
context: From document: The Graphic Novel Writing Guide for Derek: Inspiration, Structure… — chunk 6 (chunk 6/22)
